ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a clear, concrete new idea—using multiword anchor facets and constructing pseudowords (by combining co-occurrence profiles and augmenting the (V+K)×V matrix S) to extend the anchor-word algorithm—which is a non-trivial methodological extension beyond single-word anchors. It differentiates its contribution from prior work by directly comparing to single-word and Gram-Schmidt anchors and interactive baselines (ITM, Utopian), and by showing gains in classification, clustering, and topic metrics as well as interactive-scale runtimes. While the approach builds on the established anchor-word framework (so not a radical departure), the multiword-anchor/pseudoword construction and its claimed interactive steering capability constitute notable and informative novelty for experts. | B(4): The paper presents a clear and non-trivial extension of the anchor-word topic-modeling framework by introducing multiword anchor facets and constructing pseudowords (via combined co-occurrence profiles) to form an augmented (V+K)×V matrix S for convex reconstruction. This differentiates it from prior single-word anchor approaches and from sampling-based interactive methods (e.g., Interactive Topic Model, Utopian), and the work includes empirical comparisons showing improved topics and interactive scalability. Because it builds directly on an existing anchor-word paradigm rather than proposing a wholly new modeling framework, the contribution is notably novel but not revolutionary.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a clear, concrete new idea—using multiword anchor facets and constructing pseudowords (by combining co-occurrence profiles and augmenting the (V+K)×V matrix S) to extend the anchor-word algorithm—which is a non-trivial methodological extension beyond single-word anchors. It differentiates its contribution from prior work by directly comparing to single-word and Gram-Schmidt anchors and interactive baselines (ITM, Utopian), and by showing gains in classification, clustering, and topic metrics as well as interactive-scale runtimes. While the approach builds on the established anchor-word framework (so not a radical departure), the multiword-anchor/pseudoword construction and its claimed interactive steering capability constitute notable and informative novelty for experts.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a clear and non-trivial extension of the anchor-word topic-modeling framework by introducing multiword anchor facets and constructing pseudowords (via combined co-occurrence profiles) to form an augmented (V+K)×V matrix S for convex reconstruction. This differentiates it from prior single-word anchor approaches and from sampling-based interactive methods (e.g., Interactive Topic Model, Utopian), and the work includes empirical comparisons showing improved topics and interactive scalability. Because it builds directly on an existing anchor-word paradigm rather than proposing a wholly new modeling framework, the contribution is notably novel but not revolutionary.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): This work fits squarely in top-tier NLP/ML venues: it proposes an algorithmic extension to the anchor-word topic-modeling framework (Tandem Anchors) aimed at interactive topic modeling, evaluates on standard corpora (20NEWS, Amazon reviews), and directly compares to relevant baselines (Interactive Topic Model, Utopian, Gram‑Schmidt anchors). The methodology and evaluation use community-expected standards (classification accuracy, adjusted Rand index, VI, topic coherence, significance metrics) with appropriate statistical tests, and the paper explicitly targets practitioners interested in scalable, interactive topic modeling. Noted limitations (use of metadata for high-quality anchors, pseudowords, V^2 runtime scaling) are implementation/assumption concerns but do not undermine its relevance to the venue’s audience. | B(4): This paper addresses interactive topic modeling, a clear NLP/ML area, and extends the anchor-word framework with multiword anchors—an approach that is directly relevant to top-tier venues. It uses standard datasets (20NEWS, Amazon), compares to appropriate baselines (single-word anchors, Gram-Schmidt anchors, Interactive Topic Model, Utopian), and evaluates with customary metrics (classification accuracy, ARI, VI, topic coherence, significance) and statistical tests. The problem formulation and evaluation align with community expectations, though concerns such as reliance on metadata for constructing high-quality tandem anchors, pseudoword interpretability, missing hyperparameter reporting, and V^2 runtime scaling suggest minor scope/engineering limitations rather than a mismatch with the venue.

The methodology and evaluation use community-expected standards (classification accuracy, adjusted Rand index, VI, topic coherence, significance metrics) with appropriate statistical tests, and the paper explicitly targets practitioners interested in scalable, interactive topic modeling. Noted limitations (use of metadata for high-quality anchors, pseudowords, V^2 runtime scaling) are implementation/assumption concerns but do not undermine its relevance to the venue’s audience.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses interactive topic modeling, a clear NLP/ML area, and extends the anchor-word framework with multiword anchors—an approach that is directly relevant to top-tier venues. It uses standard datasets (20NEWS, Amazon), compares to appropriate baselines (single-word anchors, Gram-Schmidt anchors, Interactive Topic Model, Utopian), and evaluates with customary metrics (classification accuracy, ARI, VI, topic coherence, significance) and statistical tests. The problem formulation and evaluation align with community expectations, though concerns such as reliance on metadata for constructing high-quality tandem anchors, pseudoword interpretability, missing hyperparameter reporting, and V^2 runtime scaling suggest minor scope/engineering limitations rather than a mismatch with the venue.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are supported by experiments on 20NEWS and Amazon with appropriate baselines (single-word anchors, Gram‑Schmidt, ITM, Utopian) and a suite of evaluation metrics (classification accuracy, ARI, VI, coherence, topic significance) plus statistical tests (Wilcoxon, D’Agostino‑Pearson, t‑tests with Bonferroni), which lends credibility to the empirical claims. The mathematical approach—forming pseudowords by combining co‑occurrence profiles and solving convex KL‑reconstruction in the anchor framework—is consistent with existing anchor‑word theory, and no explicit incorrect derivations are reported in the summary. Minor technical concerns remain (missing hyperparameter reporting, the admitted inconsistency of some combiners with the extreme‑point interpretation of anchors, and V^2 runtime scaling), but these are implementation/replicability and scalability issues that do not fatally undermine the main conclusions. | B(4): The main claims are supported by empirical evidence: experiments compare tandem anchors against single-word anchors and interactive baselines (ITM, Utopian) on 20NEWS and Amazon with appropriate metrics (accuracy, ARI, VI, coherence, significance) and statistical tests (Wilcoxon, D’Agostino-Pearson, t-tests with Bonferroni). The mathematical approach (constructing pseudowords from co-occurrence profiles and solving KL-based convex reconstructions, then recovering A via Bayes’ rule) is described and appears consistent with the anchor-word framework. Minor technical issues remain: hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, tandem anchors rely on a metadata high-water mark and produce pseudowords that do not correspond to real vocabulary items, and the runtime scaling (Q construction ∝ V^2) may limit practicality—none of which appear to overturn the main experimental conclusions.

The mathematical approach—forming pseudowords by combining co‑occurrence profiles and solving convex KL‑reconstruction in the anchor framework—is consistent with existing anchor‑word theory, and no explicit incorrect derivations are reported in the summary. Minor technical concerns remain (missing hyperparameter reporting, the admitted inconsistency of some combiners with the extreme‑point interpretation of anchors, and V^2 runtime scaling), but these are implementation/replicability and scalability issues that do not fatally undermine the main conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are supported by empirical evidence: experiments compare tandem anchors against single-word anchors and interactive baselines (ITM, Utopian) on 20NEWS and Amazon with appropriate metrics (accuracy, ARI, VI, coherence, significance) and statistical tests (Wilcoxon, D’Agostino-Pearson, t-tests with Bonferroni). The mathematical approach (constructing pseudowords from co-occurrence profiles and solving KL-based convex reconstructions, then recovering A via Bayes’ rule) is described and appears consistent with the anchor-word framework. Minor technical issues remain: hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, tandem anchors rely on a metadata high-water mark and produce pseudowords that do not correspond to real vocabulary items, and the runtime scaling (Q construction ∝ V^2) may limit practicality—none of which appear to overturn the main experimental conclusions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines including single-word anchors, unsupervised Gram–Schmidt anchors, the Interactive Topic Model (Hu et al., 2014), and Utopian (Choo et al., 2013). Comparisons use the same datasets (20NEWS, Amazon) and a wide set of evaluation metrics (classification accuracy, ARI, VI, topic coherence, significance) with statistical tests reported, supporting fairness. The authors acknowledge limitations of their approach and discuss related work and runtime tradeoffs, and differences versus baselines (e.g., sampling-based ITM) are analyzed and timed. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (single-word anchors, Gram-Schmidt anchors, Interactive Topic Model (Hu et al., 2014), Utopian (Choo et al., 2013)) and evaluates them on shared datasets (20NEWS, Amazon) with multiple metrics (classification accuracy, ARI, VI, coherence, topic significance) and statistical tests, supporting fair comparisons. It also reports runtime comparisons versus sampling-based interactive methods. However, the summary does not indicate explicit acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably or better, so while comparisons and analysis are strong and related work coverage is adequate, that admission/nuance appears missing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines including single-word anchors, unsupervised Gram–Schmidt anchors, the Interactive Topic Model (Hu et al., 2014), and Utopian (Choo et al., 2013). Comparisons use the same datasets (20NEWS, Amazon) and a wide set of evaluation metrics (classification accuracy, ARI, VI, topic coherence, significance) with statistical tests reported, supporting fairness. The authors acknowledge limitations of their approach and discuss related work and runtime tradeoffs, and differences versus baselines (e.g., sampling-based ITM) are analyzed and timed.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (single-word anchors, Gram-Schmidt anchors, Interactive Topic Model (Hu et al., 2014), Utopian (Choo et al., 2013)) and evaluates them on shared datasets (20NEWS, Amazon) with multiple metrics (classification accuracy, ARI, VI, coherence, topic significance) and statistical tests, supporting fair comparisons. It also reports runtime comparisons versus sampling-based interactive methods. However, the summary does not indicate explicit acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably or better, so while comparisons and analysis are strong and related work coverage is adequate, that admission/nuance appears missing.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper provides a solid empirical evaluation against relevant baselines (single-word anchors, Gram-Schmidt anchors, Interactive Topic Model, Utopian) using multiple metrics (classification accuracy, ARI, VI, coherence, topic significance) and performs statistical tests, which supports many claims. However, experiments appear limited in dataset diversity (primarily 20NEWS and an illustrative Amazon set), and the summary indicates no reported hyperparameters or code, reducing reproducibility. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is only partially addressed (discussion of combiners and their inconsistency) but no comprehensive hyperparameter or combiner ablation is reported. The related work coverage is adequate and several limitations are acknowledged (metadata dependence, invented pseudowords, runtime scaling). | B(4): The experiments are fairly comprehensive: Tandem Anchors are compared to single-word anchors, unsupervised Gram-Schmidt anchors, ITM and Utopian on tasks (20NEWS, Amazon) using classification accuracy, adjusted Rand index, VI, coherence, and topic-significance metrics with appropriate statistical tests. Ablations/sensitivity are partially addressed (different combiners were examined and inconsistencies noted) but important reproduction details are missing (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned) and runtime scaling (Q construction ~ V^2) may merit further empirical sensitivity analysis. Related work coverage is described as adequate and limitations/failure modes (metadata dependence, pseudoword interpretation, combiner inconsistency, runtime) are explicitly discussed, supporting the claims with minor gaps in reproducibility and broader sensitivity testing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a solid empirical evaluation against relevant baselines (single-word anchors, Gram-Schmidt anchors, Interactive Topic Model, Utopian) using multiple metrics (classification accuracy, ARI, VI, coherence, topic significance) and performs statistical tests, which supports many claims. However, experiments appear limited in dataset diversity (primarily 20NEWS and an illustrative Amazon set), and the summary indicates no reported hyperparameters or code, reducing reproducibility. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is only partially addressed (discussion of combiners and their inconsistency) but no comprehensive hyperparameter or combiner ablation is reported. The related work coverage is adequate and several limitations are acknowledged (metadata dependence, invented pseudowords, runtime scaling).

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are fairly comprehensive: Tandem Anchors are compared to single-word anchors, unsupervised Gram-Schmidt anchors, ITM and Utopian on tasks (20NEWS, Amazon) using classification accuracy, adjusted Rand index, VI, coherence, and topic-significance metrics with appropriate statistical tests. Ablations/sensitivity are partially addressed (different combiners were examined and inconsistencies noted) but important reproduction details are missing (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned) and runtime scaling (Q construction ~ V^2) may merit further empirical sensitivity analysis. Related work coverage is described as adequate and limitations/failure modes (metadata dependence, pseudoword interpretation, combiner inconsistency, runtime) are explicitly discussed, supporting the claims with minor gaps in reproducibility and broader sensitivity testing.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a practical and important problem for interactive topic modeling by extending anchor-word methods to multiword ‘tandem’ anchors and empirically demonstrating higher-quality topics (better classification accuracy, clustering metrics, coherence and significance) compared to single-word anchors. Its claim of being fast and scalable enough for interactive use and faster than sampling-based interactive approaches (Interactive Topic Model, Utopian) makes it likely to change how practitioners build steerable topic models and to be adopted in interactive workflows. The method also opens research directions around richer anchor constructions and pseudoword use for steering. However, reliance on metadata to construct high-quality tandem anchors and the invented nature of pseudowords limit its immediate broad applicability, so it is impactful but not transformative across NLP. | B(4): The paper tackles the important problem of making interactive topic modeling more effective and user-friendly by introducing multiword (tandem) anchors, and demonstrates concrete gains in classification accuracy, clustering metrics, topic coherence/significance, and statistical significance over single-word anchors and unsupervised baselines. It also claims practical benefits for interactive use—being faster than sampling-based interactive models (Interactive Topic Model, Utopian) and enabling more intuitive steering via multiword anchor facets—so it could change how practitioners employ anchor-based methods. The impact is tempered by limitations noted in the summary (reliance on metadata to build high-quality tandem anchors, invented pseudowords, and V^2 scaling of the Q construction), which constrain generality but do not undermine the contribution’s usefulness for the interactive-topic-modelling subfield.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a practical and important problem for interactive topic modeling by extending anchor-word methods to multiword ‘tandem’ anchors and empirically demonstrating higher-quality topics (better classification accuracy, clustering metrics, coherence and significance) compared to single-word anchors. Its claim of being fast and scalable enough for interactive use and faster than sampling-based interactive approaches (Interactive Topic Model, Utopian) makes it likely to change how practitioners build steerable topic models and to be adopted in interactive workflows. The method also opens research directions around richer anchor constructions and pseudoword use for steering. However, reliance on metadata to construct high-quality tandem anchors and the invented nature of pseudowords limit its immediate broad applicability, so it is impactful but not transformative across NLP.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of making interactive topic modeling more effective and user-friendly by introducing multiword (tandem) anchors, and demonstrates concrete gains in classification accuracy, clustering metrics, topic coherence/significance, and statistical significance over single-word anchors and unsupervised baselines. It also claims practical benefits for interactive use—being faster than sampling-based interactive models (Interactive Topic Model, Utopian) and enabling more intuitive steering via multiword anchor facets—so it could change how practitioners employ anchor-based methods. The impact is tempered by limitations noted in the summary (reliance on metadata to build high-quality tandem anchors, invented pseudowords, and V^2 scaling of the Q construction), which constrain generality but do not undermine the contribution’s usefulness for the interactive-topic-modelling subfield.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and the main contributions (Tandem Anchors, pseudoword construction, and claims about interactivity and scalability) are stated early; the method notation (S, C, A matrices and KL-based reconstruction) is consistent and explained. Figures, tables, and examples supporting experiments (20NEWS, Amazon; baselines and metrics) are informative and the writing quality is reported as good with adequate related-work coverage. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported and the use of invented pseudowords could benefit from additional illustrative examples or clarification. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from introduction through method (pseudoword construction and convex reconstruction) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (Tandem Anchors, multiword anchor facets, and interactive use cases) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and well-defined for the extended anchor-word algorithm, and the use of datasets (20NEWS, Amazon examples) and quantitative metrics makes the examples and experimental presentation informative. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate. A minor presentation omission is that hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, which reduces reproducibility and clarity for readers wanting to replicate results.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and the main contributions (Tandem Anchors, pseudoword construction, and claims about interactivity and scalability) are stated early; the method notation (S, C, A matrices and KL-based reconstruction) is consistent and explained. Figures, tables, and examples supporting experiments (20NEWS, Amazon; baselines and metrics) are informative and the writing quality is reported as good with adequate related-work coverage. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported and the use of invented pseudowords could benefit from additional illustrative examples or clarification.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear flow from introduction through method (pseudoword construction and convex reconstruction) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (Tandem Anchors, multiword anchor facets, and interactive use cases) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and well-defined for the extended anchor-word algorithm, and the use of datasets (20NEWS, Amazon examples) and quantitative metrics makes the examples and experimental presentation informative. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate. A minor presentation omission is that hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, which reduces reproducibility and clarity for readers wanting to replicate results.

main_claims: Proposes Tandem Anchors: using multiword anchor facets (sets of words) as anchors in the anchor-word topic modeling framework.

Tandem anchors produce higher-quality topics than single-word anchors for interactive topic modeling, yielding higher classification accuracy, improved clustering metrics, and more significant topics.
Tandem anchors are fast and scalable enough for interactive use and are faster than sampling-based interactive topic-modeling approaches (e.g., Interactive Topic Model, Utopian).
Tandem anchors make anchor-based topic modeling more intuitive and enable users to steer topics via multiword anchors.
_summary.methodology: Extend the anchor-word algorithm by constructing pseudowords from multiword anchor facets via combining their co-occurrence profiles to form an augmented (V+K)×V matrix S, then for each word solve a KL-divergence based convex-reconstruction (using the pseudowords as anchors) to obtain coefficients C and recover topic-word distributions A via Bayes' rule.
baselines: Fast ITM / optimized ITM timings cited
metrics: statistical tests: Wilcoxon signed-rank, D'Agostino-Pearson normality, t-tests with Bonferroni correction
limitations: Cannot assume metadata will be available to build high-quality tandem anchors (used metadata as a high-water mark).
Pseudowords do not correspond to real vocabulary items (invented co-occurrence profiles).
Some combiners (e.g., vector average) are inconsistent with the interpretation that anchors should be extreme points.
Anchor-method runtime depends on the square of vocabulary size (Q construction scales with V^2).
